text.tspan: Producer blocks (or async-awaits) when queue is full.
Natural in Go channels, Java BlockingQueue, Rust mpsc::sync_channel.
Simple and correct. Couples producer's rate to consumer's rate.
When queue is full, drop messages (reject incoming requests with 503).
Lose some work; protect the system. Often best for stateless requests.
VARIANTS: random drop, tail drop, head drop, prioritized drop (drop low priority first).
Producer doesn't push at will; consumer requests N items at a time.
USED BY: Reactive Streams spec (request(n) signal), Kafka consumer poll(),
ReactiveX (RxJava with backpressure), Project Reactor, RSocket.
Producer has no choice but to respect consumer's rate; natural backpressure.
Consumer grants 'credits' (sliding window) to producer.
Producer can send up to credit limit; consumer replenishes as it processes.
USED BY: TCP receive window; HTTP/2 stream flow control; gRPC flow control;
Reactive Streams .request(n) mechanism.
Producer auto-adjusts rate based on observed signals:
u2022 success u2192 increase rate by +1
u2022 timeout / failure / 503 u2192 cut rate in half
USED BY: TCP congestion control, AWS SDK retry, adaptive rate limiters.
On rejection / timeout / 429: wait N seconds and retry; N doubles each time;
add random jitter to prevent synchronized retries (thundering retry herd).
Not exactly backpressure, but the complement: consumers respond to push-back signals.
